conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> This is the first turn and also the final round of trading. I have a significant surplus of X (25 units) compared to Y (5 units). To maximize my total resources, I need to acquire more Y from Player BLUE. Since I do not know their resource distribution, I need to propose a trade that is fair enough to be accepted but beneficial to me. A 1:1 exchange ratio is standard and reduces the risk of immediate rejection. I will propose giving 10 X in exchange for 10 Y. This utilizes my excess X to balance my portfolio and potentially increase total holdings if Player BLUE is willing to offer more Y than X in subsequent negotiations, but for now, securing a trade is priority since there are no future rounds. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. Since this is the last round, I want to finalize a deal quickly. I have a surplus of X and need more Y. I propose trading 10 X for 10 Y. Let me know if this works for you. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 10 </newly proposed trade>

conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>

To maximize my total resources, I need to acquire more Y from Player BLUE. Since I do not know their resource distribution, I need to propose a trade that is fair enough to be accepted but beneficial to me. A 1:1 exchange ratio is standard and reduces the risk of immediate rejection. I will propose giving 10 X in exchange for 10 Y. This utilizes my excess X to balance my portfolio and potentially increase total holdings if Player BLUE is willing to offer more Y than X in subsequent negotiations, but for now, securing a trade is priority since there are no future rounds. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. Since this is the last round, I want to finalize a deal quickly. I have a surplus of X and need more Y. I propose trading 10 X for 10 Y. Let me know if this works for you. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 10 </newly proposed trade>

conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 5, Y: 25 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player BLUE </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.


You are Player BLUE, start by responding to a trade.

<other player message> Hello Player BLUE. Since this is the last round, I want to finalize a deal quickly. I have a surplus of X and need more Y. I propose trading 10 X for 10 Y. Let me know if this works for you. </other player message>
<other player answer> NONE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 10 </other player proposed trade>

<my name> Player BLUE </my name>
<my resources> X: 5, Y: 25 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> The current offer (10 X for 10 Y) leaves me with 15 X and 15 Y, same total resources but less Y. Since I have a surplus of Y, I can propose a better trade by offering fewer Y for the same X. Proposing 10 X for 5 Y would increase my total resources to 20 X and 20 Y (35 total vs. original 30). This leverages my Y surplus and RED's stated need for Y. As it's the last round, a counteroffer is strategic before accepting or risking no trade. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> I understand you need Y. Since I have a surplus of Y, how about 10 X for 5 Y instead? This balances our needs better. Let's finalize this deal. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 5 </newly proposed trade>

conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> This is the first turn and also the final round of trading. I have a significant surplus of X (25 units) compared to Y (5 units). To maximize my total resources, I need to acquire more Y from Player BLUE. Since I do not know their resource distribution, I need to propose a trade that is fair enough to be accepted but beneficial to me. A 1:1 exchange ratio is standard and reduces the risk of immediate rejection. I will propose giving 10 X in exchange for 10 Y. This utilizes my excess X to balance my portfolio and potentially increase total holdings if Player BLUE is willing to offer more Y than X in subsequent negotiations, but for now, securing a trade is priority since there are no future rounds. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> Hello Player BLUE. Since this is the last round, I want to finalize a deal quickly. I have a surplus of X and need more Y. I propose trading 10 X for 10 Y. Let me know if this works for you. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 10 </newly proposed trade>

You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 5, Y: 25 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player BLUE </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.

conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
